title: Moonwalking with Einstein: The Art and Science of Remembering Everything
excerpt: A journalist's yearlong journey from average memory to winning the US Memory Championship, revealing ancient techniques and cutting-edge science of remembering.
keyIdeas: Extraordinary memory is a skill that can be learned, not a genetic gift
Ancient memory techniques like the method of loci (memory palace) are still the most effective
Memory is deeply tied to visualization, spatial navigation, and creative association
What we remember is what we pay attention to—memory is a byproduct of engagement
keyTakeaways: Build memory palaces to remember lists, speeches, and complex information
Transform abstract information into vivid, memorable images
Practice memory techniques consistently to see dramatic improvement
Understand that a better memory leads to deeper learning and understanding
whoShouldRead: Anyone who wants to improve their memory for practical daily use
Students looking for better study and recall techniques
Professionals who need to remember large amounts of information
Fans of narrative nonfiction and immersive journalism
whoShouldSkip: Those seeking purely scientific analysis without personal narrative
Readers who find memory competitions uninteresting or gimmicky
